item.title: Just found this after almost a year...
item.content:encoded:
…and i am baffled it only is at +30 (excluding me) as of now.

In the future, i will take this article as example that one can take an old, apprently uninteresting concept ("ghosts") and turn it into something wonderfull with the right narrative.

Take my upvote, i'll make sure to spread the word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like others said, the clinical tone in this article is amazing. I personally also liked the ending, it’s an easy way to boost D-class numbers and makes the foundation seem more cold and calculating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly by the standards of D-Class recruitment this is probably one of the more justified ones. They know he murdered someone brutally for fairly shitty reasons.
Why wouldn't he end up a D?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing I dislike about this article is the Person of Interest being turned into a D-Class at the very end. The narrative could very much do without, and I don't see how the Foundation's solution to this is justified. It skips the "cold, not cruel" mentality we so often see described (a theme which has proven to be rather useful) and makes the research team rather cruel in their own right. This criticism could perhaps be the result of a headcanon conflict, but the portrayal as it is depicted doesn't convince me.

As well, how would the father's apparition act or behave if he knew his son was locked up in the same kind of facility he was? Doesn't that go against the information gleaned in Addendum 2, about not pissing off the Dead? I don't think Kevin's actions should go unpunished, but I also don't think the way the Foundation handles this is appropriate. Now, I guess it is possible that the D-Class ending is just an extension of the article's theme — that actions have unfair punishments — but I digress.

Criticisms aside. I think this is decently well-written, and while I can agree with Yossi above that the dialogue needs extra work to sound more realistic, I can sympathise with the characters and story being told. So that's my +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding Malice here. There's an engaging premise with the imagery and I did get a bit of an emotional impact at some points, but it was leaning far too hard on telling rather than showing, and the fairly straightforward ghost plot didn't do it any favors.

Novoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did not see that coming. Profoundly disturbing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, I felt the researcher's reactions to the PoI just weren't realistic to some degree.

Well, I figured being employed by the Foundation would expose you to stuff much worse than this but take with it as you will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like it. The clinical tone is superb, but the dialogue could use some touching up (specifically the last interview). Also, I felt the researcher's reactions to the PoI just weren't realistic to some degree.

However, this is still well written. The emotion came across, and I can't help but feel pity for the characters. Have my upvote. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mhm. This is… an odd one. I really like ghost stories, and things that play with that concept in the clinical setting of the Foundation, but this… I'm not sure. It's a fairly standard ghost premise (not that that's a bad thing!), but the entire story is essentially conveyed through expository dialogue, and the characters aren't really engaging or unique enough for that to pay off on its own. I was excited to hear about how the wounds came about — they're weird — but the ending felt like a let down after the buildup relating to the Foundation's internal management of ghosts, which in itself is something I'd be really interested to read about.

You start off with strong apparition imagery, but it gets bogged down with straightforward "here's what happened" interviews, a fairly irrelevant tidbit about "Apparition Escalation: Preliminary Research into Human Triggered Escalation of Apparition Hostility" — which, again, I'd love to be a more major part of the article — and characters that just seem… flat.

Sorry, man; gonna have to novote this one. It's not bad, but the story being told here is pretty much the most straightforward ghost story you could come up with. Given the imagery you came up with for stuff like 4099, I'm certain you've got it in you to do the concept justice.

+ Grammar/wording nitpicks
– hide block

Should SCP-4435 begin exhibiting unnatural behavior, the chamber is to be kept at a high setting to prevent SCP-4435 from dematerializing.

"kept" feels like a weird word choice here. I feel like it should be "set at" or "turned to" or something; 'kept' implies a permanence that goes against the "unnatural behaviour" line.

Specifically, the Coracohumeral and Capsular ligaments of the shoulder, the Palmar Radiocarpal ligaments in the wrists, and the lateral collateral ligaments and the Patelllar tendons near the Cruciate ligaments for the knee joints.

You don't need to capitalise these terms.

it has shown to fully change to either one of these states

I think you're missing a word in this clause.

SCP-4435 was discovered by PoI-9009 ("Kevin Nguyen") after the subject had reported a "supernatural presence"

This works without the word "had", and its inclusion feels weird to me.

Under no circumstances are D-9009 and SCP-4435 be in the same room together.

Again, a missing word.

item.title: Hey
item.content:encoded:
"For the sins of your fathers you, though guiltless, must suffer."

- Horace, Odes, Book III

"One day you will do things for me that you hate. That is what it means to be a family."

- Jonathan Safran Foer

This is an article that's a little personal. It's about Anger, Hatred, Guilt, Regrets, and Family. I'll probably be taking a bit of a break from SCP Writing. I just have a lot going on my plate at the moment.

Thanks to Wydness and Veiedhimaedhr for looking at this.

Source: https://www.flickr.com/photos/robadob/353034058/
